summary: Tractate Yoma daf 87b covers: Rav went to appease R. Hiyya; R. Hiyya refused; the appeasement story; Main mitzva of vidui (confession) is on Yom Kippur; also recited at Minha in case something happens; Closing of tractate Yoma: Hadran formula; reference to Tu BeAv and Yom Kippur as the best days.

display.whats: Rav went to appease R. Hiyya; R. Hiyya refused; the appeasement story
display.hint: The Gemara tells the story of Rav going to R. Hiyya (his uncle) to seek appeasement after having offended him. R. Hiyya repeatedly refused to be appeased, and Rav ultimately left. The story illustrate...
display.title: Rav Seeks Appeasement from R. Hiyya

learning.ahaMoment: Real repair requires both parties: Rav's persistence shows that appeasement is not a speech act - it is a relational process that only completes when the other person accepts.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rav went to R. Hiyya again and again: persistence is part of the appeasement obligation, not just one attempt.
learning.learnerQuestion: What does the story of Rav seeking appeasement from his uncle R. Hiyya teach about the practice of interpersonal repair?
learning.coreTension: Between the principle (you must appease the person you wronged) and the reality of what happens when the wronged person refuses or is unavailable to accept the apology.
learning.coreMove: The story records that Rav went to R. Hiyya multiple times to seek appeasement. R. Hiyya's initial refusal raises the question: does one failed attempt satisfy the obligation? The Gemara's account of multiple attempts illustrates that genuine appeasement requires persistence, not just a single formulaic approach.

takeaway.text: The story of Rav and R. Hiyya illustrates that the appeasement obligation requires genuine effort and persistence; a single refused attempt does not complete the obligation, and the wronged person's acceptance - not just the sinner's approach - is part of the process.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume one sincere apology completes the obligation; the story shows that the wronged person's reception is also part of the halakhic account.

argumentFlow.label: Rav restarts the reading; Rabbi Chanina is slighted
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: As Rav read before Rabbi, he restarted the portion each time a senior arrived - for Rabbi Chiyya, bar Kappara, and Rabbi Shimon son of Rabbi. When Rabbi Chanina bar Chama then arrived, Rav thought 'shall I keep going back?' and continued without restarting. Rabbi Chanina was offended at being ranked below the others, and Rav went to appease him every Yom Kippur eve for thirteen years, but he would not relent.

argumentFlow.label: Two objections from the rules of appeasement
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara objects two ways. How could Rav ask more than the three times Rabbi Yosei bar Chanina prescribed? Answer: Rav was exceptionally pious and went beyond the letter of the law. And how could Rabbi Chanina refuse, when Rava taught that whoever forgoes his honor and forgives has all his sins pardoned?

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Chanina's hidden motive
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The resolution: Rabbi Chanina had dreamt Rav was hanged on a palm tree, a tradition signaling Rav would become head of a yeshiva. Knowing two reigns cannot overlap, he deliberately withheld forgiveness so Rav would leave for Babylonia and head a yeshiva there - fulfilling the dream without costing Rabbi Chanina his own position.

theological: R. Hiyya refusing appeasement: the obligation to ask is on the wrongdoer; the ob
narrative: Rav went to appease R. Hiyya; R. Hiyya refused; the appeasem

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Rabbi Chanina simply held a grudge and violated the duty to forgive.
misconceptions.correction: His refusal was strategic and providential: he foresaw Rav's destined leadership and withheld forgiveness so Rav would relocate to Babylonia to head a yeshiva there, not out of mere resentment.

visualizableElements.name: Rav went to appease R. Hiyya; R. Hiyya refused; th

quizSeeds.question: Why did Rav appease Rabbi Chanina for thirteen years when the rule is to ask only three times?
quizSeeds.answer: Because Rav was exceptionally pious and acted beyond the letter of the law (lifnim mishurat hadin). The three-time limit is a floor of obligation, not a ceiling on devotion.

quizSeeds.question: What was Rabbi Chanina's real reason for never forgiving Rav?
quizSeeds.answer: He had dreamt Rav would head a yeshiva, and knowing two reigns cannot overlap, he withheld forgiveness so Rav would go to Babylonia and lead a yeshiva there - fulfilling the dream without Rabbi Chanina losing his own position.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara tells the story of Rav going to R. Hiyya (his uncle) to seek appeasement after having offended him. R. Hiyya repeatedly refused to be appea

display.whats: Main mitzva of vidui (confession) is on Yom Kippur; also recited at Minha in case something happens
display.hint: The primary performance of vidui (verbal confession) on Yom Kippur is at the Minha prayer. If a person recited vidui at Minha but dies before YK, they have fulfilled the mitzva. Vidui is also recited ...
display.title: Vidui: Primary at Minha, Optional Earlier

learning.ahaMoment: The Minha timing is practical wisdom: if something goes wrong at the pre-fast meal, the confession was already made. The timing protects the person against the risk of dying un-confessed.
learning.memoryAnchor: Vidui primary at Minha on Erev YK: timed before the meal to ensure confession is completed even if something goes wrong.
learning.learnerQuestion: When is the primary time for the vidui (confession) on Yom Kippur, and is it permitted to confess earlier?
learning.coreTension: Between the primary time for vidui (Minha, the afternoon prayer of Erev YK) and the permissibility of confessing earlier in the day, during other prayers or at other times.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara establishes that the primary mitzvah of vidui on YK is fulfilled at Minha (so that if something happens at the meal before YK the confession has been made), but earlier confession is also valid and the Gemara records that it was recited at multiple prayer times.

takeaway.text: The primary time for YK vidui is Minha on Erev YK, timed to precede the final meal so that confession has been made even if something goes wrong at the meal; earlier recitation is also valid and practiced.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume all confessions are equivalent regardless of timing; the Gemara's attention to the Minha timing reveals a specific concern about ensuring the confession is made before the meal.

argumentFlow.label: The timing of Yom Kippur confession
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: The Sages taught: the core confession is at nightfall as Yom Kippur begins. But the Sages added one should also confess before the pre-fast meal, lest the food and drink confuse him afterward. And though he confessed before eating, he confesses again after, in case he sinned during the meal. And he confesses at each prayer - evening, morning, musaf, mincha, and ne'ila - layering confession through the day.

argumentFlow.label: Where in the prayer, and what text
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara / Amoraim
argumentFlow.text: An individual says the confession after his Amida; the prayer leader says it within the Amida. As for the text: Rav opens 'You know the mysteries of the universe'; Shmuel, 'From the depths of the heart'; Levi, 'And in Your Torah it is written'; Rabbi Yochanan, 'Master of the Universe' - a range of received openings.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Hamnuna's confession
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yehuda / Rav Hamnuna
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yehuda's text: 'our iniquities are too many to count, our sins too great to number.' Rav Hamnuna's: 'My God, before I was formed I was unworthy... I am dust in life, all the more so in death... a vessel full of shame... may I sin no more, and erase my sins in Your compassion, not through suffering.' This was Rava's confession all year and Rav Hamnuna Zuta's on Yom Kippur.

argumentFlow.label: The essential phrase: 'aval anachnu chatanu'
argumentFlow.speaker: Mar Zutra
argumentFlow.text: Mar Zutra: all these texts are needed only if one did not say 'but we have sinned (aval anachnu chatanu)' - that phrase is the essence of the confession and suffices on its own. Bar Hamdudei testified that Shmuel stood when the leader reached those words, marking them as the heart of the vidui.

theological: Vidui at Minha: confession is prioritized before YK begins, while the person is
narrative: Main mitzva of vidui (confession) is on Yom Kippur; also rec

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: One only confesses once on Yom Kippur, at the start of the fast.
misconceptions.correction: The confession is layered: before and after the pre-fast meal, and at every one of the day's prayers - evening, morning, musaf, mincha, and ne'ila - to cover sins committed at each stage.

visualizableElements.name: Main mitzva of vidui (confession) is on Yom Kippur

quizSeeds.question: Why did the Sages add a confession before the pre-Yom-Kippur meal in addition to the one at nightfall?
quizSeeds.answer: Lest the abundance of food and drink at the meal leave a person confused and unable to confess afterward. He confesses before the meal as a safeguard, then again after it in case he sinned during the meal.

quizSeeds.question: According to Mar Zutra, what is the essential part of the confession?
quizSeeds.answer: The phrase 'but we have sinned' (aval anachnu chatanu). The various longer texts are needed only if one did not say it; that phrase alone suffices, which is why Shmuel would stand when the leader reached it.

sugyot.finalRuling: The primary performance of vidui (verbal confession) on Yom Kippur is at the Minha prayer. If a person recited vidui at Minha but dies before YK, they

display.whats: Closing of tractate Yoma: Hadran formula; reference to Tu BeAv and Yom Kippur as the best days
display.hint: The tractate closes with the Hadran formula (a declaration of return to the tractate) and with the famous statement that the most joyful days for Israel are Tu BeAv and Yom Kippur.
display.title: Closing of Tractate Yoma: Hadran and Tu BeAv

learning.ahaMoment: The tractate ends pointing toward joy. Atonement is not the destination - it is the passage. After YK comes Tu BeAv: after the seriousness of repair comes the celebration of renewed connection.
learning.memoryAnchor: Yoma ends with Hadran and Tu BeAv: from atonement's gravity to celebration's joy - repair leads to renewal.
learning.learnerQuestion: What is the significance of the Hadran formula and the reference to Tu BeAv at the close of Tractate Yoma?
learning.coreTension: Between the heavy topic of Yom Kippur (atonement, death, sin) and the closing reference to Tu BeAv, a day of celebration and matchmaking.
learning.coreMove: The tractate closes with the Hadran (a declaration of return - 'we will return to you'), expressing that the learner and the tractate maintain a mutual relationship across time. The reference to Tu BeAv marks the transition from the gravity of YK to the joy of reconnection - from atonement's completion to life's renewal.

takeaway.text: The closing of Tractate Yoma with the Hadran and a reference to joy (Tu BeAv) expresses the rabbinic view that atonement leads to renewal, not just to relief; the tractate that dwelt on sin and death ends by pointing toward life and celebration.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may see the Hadran as a formality; the content of the Hadran (mutual fidelity between learner and text) and its placement here carry theological meaning about the nature of Torah study.

argumentFlow.label: When the priestly blessing is said four times a day
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah (Ta'anit)
argumentFlow.text: A Mishnah in Ta'anit: three times a year the priests bless four times in one day - shacharit, musaf, mincha, and ne'ila. These three occasions are communal fasts for rain (which have ne'ila), non-priestly watches (ma'amadot), and Yom Kippur. This introduces ne'ila as a distinct prayer.

argumentFlow.label: Is ne'ila a full Amida or just a confession?
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav / Shmuel
argumentFlow.text: What is ne'ila? Rav: an added (full) Amida prayer. Shmuel: not a full prayer, only a confession beginning 'what are we, what are our lives?' A baraita listing seven blessings 'and confesses' at ne'ila supports Rav that it is a full Amida.

argumentFlow.label: The dispute refined; Shmuel refuted
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara recasts it: all agree ne'ila is a full prayer; the tannaitic dispute is only whether one must confess at ne'ila (Rabbi Meir requires ending with confession; the Rabbis make it optional). Since all hold ne'ila is a complete prayer, Shmuel's view that it is merely a confession is conclusively refuted.

argumentFlow.label: Ulla bar Rav leads ne'ila
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Ulla bar Rav led ne'ila before Rava, opening 'You have chosen us' and closing 'what are we, what are our lives?' - and Rava praised him. Rav Huna son of Rav Natan adds that an individual says the confession after his Amida, not within it as the leader does.

argumentFlow.label: Rav: ne'ila exempts the evening prayer
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav
argumentFlow.text: Rav: reciting ne'ila exempts one from the evening (ma'ariv) prayer, since the added prayer is recited as darkness falls and serves as the evening prayer - consistent with Rav's view that ne'ila is an added Amida.

argumentFlow.label: But Rav said ma'ariv is optional!
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Objection: didn't Rav rule that the evening prayer is optional? Then why say ne'ila 'exempts' it - one is exempt anyway? Answer: Rav's statement here follows the opinion that ma'ariv is mandatory; even on that view, ne'ila fulfills the evening obligation.

argumentFlow.label: A baraita adds an abridged evening Amida
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A further baraita is raised: after Yom Kippur one prays the seven blessings of each service and confesses, and then an abridged eighteen-blessing evening Amida - implying ne'ila does NOT exempt ma'ariv. Rabbi Chanina ben Gamliel in his ancestors' name says one prays the full eighteen as usual, the discussion continuing on 88a regarding havdala.

theological: YK as a day of joy: the tractate ends with YK called one of the most joyful days
narrative: Closing of tractate Yoma: Hadran formula; reference to Tu Be

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Ne'ila is essentially just the closing confession of Yom Kippur.
misconceptions.correction: That was Shmuel's view, but it is conclusively refuted. All the tannaim treat ne'ila as a full seven-blessing Amida; they dispute only whether a confession must be appended to it.

visualizableElements.name: Closing of tractate Yoma: Hadran formula; referenc

quizSeeds.question: What is the dispute between Rav and Shmuel about the nature of ne'ila, and how is it resolved?
quizSeeds.answer: Rav holds ne'ila is a full added Amida; Shmuel holds it is only a confession ('what are we, what are our lives?'). Shmuel is conclusively refuted: all the tannaim treat ne'ila as a complete prayer, disputing only whether confession at ne'ila is required.

quizSeeds.question: How does Rav reconcile saying ne'ila 'exempts' the evening prayer with his view that ma'ariv is optional?
quizSeeds.answer: His statement that ne'ila exempts ma'ariv is made according to the opinion that the evening prayer is mandatory. Even on that view, reciting ne'ila as darkness falls fulfills the evening obligation.

sugyot.finalRuling: The tractate closes with the Hadran formula (a declaration of return to the tractate) and with the famous statement that the most joyful days for Isra

rashiTranslations.he: אתא ר' חייא. וגם הוא היה חפץ לפסוק עמו וחזר בשבילו לראש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the story of Rav and R. Hiyya - the wrongdoer must attempt appeasement.

rashiTranslations.he: הפרשה: רב שאני. מחמיר היה לעצמו: חלמא חזא ליה לרב. ראה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the primary time for vidui (confession) is the Minha prayer before YK.

rashiTranslations.he: חלום על רב שתלאוהו בדקל והוא סימן נשיאות ראש וגדולה ור'

rashiTranslations.he: חנינא ראש ישיבה היה כדאמר רבי (כתובות דף קג:) בשעת פטירתו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: Tu BeAv and Yom Kippur as the most joyful days for the Jewish people.

Rashi notes that the Amoraim in the Yerushalmi (tractate Berakhot) dispute the meaning of 'ne'ila' - what exactly is being 'locked' or closed in this final prayer.

rashiTranslations.he: ירושלמי חד אמר נעילת שערי שמים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: one opinion in the Yerushalmi says 'ne'ila' refers to the locking of the gates of Heaven - the prayer is timed as the Heavenly gates close at the end of Yom Kippur.

rashiTranslations.he: וחד אמר נעילת שערי עזרה: וכיון
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the other opinion says 'ne'ila' refers to the locking of the Temple courtyard gates (sha'arei azarah) at dusk. He then transitions to: 'once he prayed' (ne'ila) - after ne'ila is recited, the evening prayer is no longer needed.

rashiTranslations.he: דצלי. משחשיכה תו לא צריך: והא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: once ne'ila has been prayed and darkness has fallen, one no longer needs the evening prayer - ne'ila has discharged that obligation. Then the Gemara raises an objection from Rav's own statement.

rashiTranslations.he: אמר רב. במס' ברכות תפלת ערבית
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi notes: Rav said in tractate Berakhot that the evening prayer (Arvit) is optional. What then does 'exempts' in our Mishnah mean?
According to the one who says Arvit is obligatory - Neilah on Yom Kippur exempts it.
That is what the Mishnah means by 'exempts' - this prayer exempts the evening obligation.
An objection is raised: the Mishnah lists Neilah separately and also lists Arvit separately.
Rashi explains 'seven like eighteen': the three opening and three closing blessings recited in full form...
...the three opening and three closing blessings as normally instituted,

rashiTranslations.he: ואומר הביננו באמצע שיש בה מעין
rashiTranslations.en: ...and one says Havinenu in the middle, which contains a condensed form of...

rashiTranslations.he: שלש עשרה ברכות והיא תפלה קצרה
rashiTranslations.en: ...the thirteen middle blessings in abbreviated form - it is a short prayer...
...enacted for travelers and for those concluding Yom Kippur, who were permitted to use it due to the burden of the fast.
Rashi then opens the comment on the next ruling regarding this leniency.
Rashi marks the opening of a tannaitic dispute regarding the above ruling.
